title: N queens basic
description: Solve N-Queens and print all solutions

examples.explanation: Step 1: Generate all permutations of [0,1,2,3]. Step 2: Check permutation [1,3,0,2]: Check pairs: (0,1): |0-1|=1, |1-3|=2 → no conflict; (0,2): |0-2|=2, |1-0|=1 → no; (0,3): |0-3|=3, |1-2|=1 → no; (1,2): |1-2|=1, |3-0|=3 → no; (1,3): |1-3|=2, |3-2|=1 → no; (2,3): |2-3|=1, |0-2|=2 → no → valid. Convert to board: row0 col1 → ".Q..", row1 col3 → "...Q", row2 col0 → "Q...", row3 col2 → "..Q.". Step 3: Check [2,0,3,1]: Similar check → valid. Board: row0 col2 → "..Q.", row1 col0 → "Q...", row2 col3 → "...Q", row3 col1 → ".Q..". Step 4: Other permutations fail diagonal checks. Total valid: 2 solutions.
Step 1: Generate permutation [0]. Step 2: Only one queen → no pairs to check → valid. Step 3: Convert to board: row0 col0 → "Q". Step 4: Output single solution.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(N!)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of queen placements on rows and check each for diagonal conflicts. Use it for small N to understand the problem, but it's inefficient due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — N-Queens Brute Force Solution
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of N-Queens using permutation generation and diagonal checking
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to N-Queens leverages the observation that queens must be in different rows and columns. By generating all permutations of column positions (0 to N-1), we ensure no two queens share the same column. Each permutation represents a potential solution where the index indicates the row and the value indicates the column. The key insight is that we only need to check diagonal conflicts since row and column conflicts are already prevented by the permutation structure. The algorithm works by: 1) Generating all permutations of [0,1,...,N-1], 2) For each permutation, checking if any two queens are on the same diagonal (absolute difference between rows equals absolute difference between columns), 3) Collecting valid permutations and converting them to board representations. The time complexity is O(N! * N²) because we generate N! permutations and for each, we check all pairs of queens (N² operations). Space complexity is O(N) for storing the current permutation and recursion stack. This approach is foundational for understanding the problem constraints but becomes impractical for N > 10 due to factorial growth. Connection to L1: This level integrates the diagonal checking subroutine (L1) with permutation generation to solve the complete problem. Common mistakes include: forgetting to convert 0-based indices to 1-based for output, inefficient diagonal checking (O(N²) instead of O(N) with sets), and not pruning invalid permutations early. Real code pattern: Use backtracking to generate permutations, check diagonals during generation to prune invalid paths early.

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate all permutations of [0,1,2,3]. Step 2: Check permutation [1,3,0,2]: Check pairs: (0,1): |0-1|=1, |1-3|=2 → no conflict; (0,2): |0-2|=2, |1-0|=1 → no; (0,3): |0-3|=3, |1-2|=1 → no; (1,2): |1-2|=1, |3-0|=3 → no; (1,3): |1-3|=2, |3-2|=1 → no; (2,3): |2-3|=1, |0-2|=2 → no → valid. Convert to board: row0 col1 → ".Q..", row1 col3 → "...Q", row2 col0 → "Q...", row3 col2 → "..Q.". Step 3: Check [2,0,3,1]: Similar check → valid. Board: row0 col2 → "..Q.", row1 col0 → "Q...", row2 col3 → "...Q", row3 col1 → ".Q..". Step 4: Other permutations fail diagonal checks. Total valid: 2 solutions.
Step 1: Generate permutation [0]. Step 2: Only one queen → no pairs to check → valid. Step 3: Convert to board: row0 col0 → "Q". Step 4: Output single solution.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Diagonal Conflict Checking
ladders.desc: Function to check if any two queens in a permutation are on the same diagonal
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force N-Queens solution. Given a permutation representing queen positions (index = row, value = column), we need to determine if the configuration has any diagonal conflicts. The intuition comes from geometry: two points (r1,c1) and (r2,c2) are on the same diagonal if |r1 - r2| = |c1 - c2|. This means the absolute difference in rows equals the absolute difference in columns. The algorithm iterates through all pairs of queens (i,j) where i < j, checking this condition. A more efficient O(N) approach uses sets to track occupied diagonals: for each queen at (row, col), compute main_diag = row - col and anti_diag = row + col. If either value already exists in their respective sets, there's a conflict. The brute O(N²) check is simpler for understanding. Time complexity: O(N²) for pairwise check, O(N) with sets. Space: O(1) or O(N) for sets. Connection to L0: This function is called for each generated permutation in the full solution. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, confusing row/column differences, not using absolute values. Real code pattern: Loop i from 0 to N-2, j from i+1 to N-1, if abs(i - j) == abs(perm[i] - perm[j]) return False.

examples.trace: Check pairs: (0,1): |0-1|=1, |0-2|=2 → no; (0,2): |0-2|=2, |0-4|=4 → no; (0,3): |0-3|=3, |0-1|=1 → no; (0,4): |0-4|=4, |0-3|=3 → no; (1,2): |1-2|=1, |2-4|=2 → no; (1,3): |1-3|=2, |2-1|=1 → no; (1,4): |1-4|=3, |2-3|=1 → no; (2,3): |2-3|=1, |4-1|=3 → no; (2,4): |2-4|=2, |4-3|=1 → no; (3,4): |3-4|=1, |1-3|=2 → no. Wait, recheck: Actually (1,3): |1-3|=2, |2-1|=1 → no conflict. But (0,2) already checked. Let's check systematically: All pairs pass? Actually, test shows conflict at (1,3) if miscalculated. Correct: For perm=[0,2,4,1,3], check (2,4): |2-4|=2, |4-3|=1 → no. Actually, let's verify with code: All pairs are valid. I made an error. Let's trace accurately: Pair (0,1): diff rows=1, diff cols=2 → no. (0,2): 2 vs 4 → no. (0,3): 3 vs 1 → no. (0,4): 4 vs 3 → no. (1,2): 1 vs 2 → no. (1,3): 2 vs 1 → no. (1,4): 3 vs 1 → no. (2,3): 1 vs 3 → no. (2,4): 2 vs 1 → no. (3,4): 1 vs 2 → no. So actually True. But the example said False. Let's use a conflicting example: perm=[0,1,2,3] for N=4. Check (0,1): 1 vs 1 → conflict! So output False. Revised example: Input perm=[0,1,2,3], output False. Trace: (0,1): |0-1|=1, |0-1|=1 → conflict → return False immediately.

testCases.expected: False (invalid permutation but check would fail)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Permutation Generation for Queen Placements
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of column indices to represent queen positions
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the brute force approach is that we can represent queen placements as permutations of column indices [0,1,...,N-1], where the index in the permutation represents the row. This automatically ensures no two queens share the same row or column. The intuition: In N-Queens, we need to place one queen per row. If we assign each queen a unique column, we have a permutation. Generating all permutations gives us all possible assignments of columns to rows. The algorithm uses backtracking: start with an empty list, recursively add unused numbers (columns) to build permutations. Pseudocode: def backtrack(path, used): if len(path)==N: add copy to results; return; for col in range(N): if col not in used: used.add(col); path.append(col); backtrack(path, used); path.pop(); used.remove(col). Time complexity: O(N!) permutations generated. Space: O(N) for recursion depth and storage. Connection to L1: Each generated permutation is passed to the diagonal checking subroutine. Common mistakes: Not copying the permutation before storing (reference issues), inefficient used set operations, not pruning. Real code pattern: Use DFS with a visited set or boolean array.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty path [], used={}. Step 2: First level: choose col=0: path=[0], used={0}. Recurse: choose col=1: path=[0,1], used={0,1}. Recurse: choose col=2: path=[0,1,2], used={0,1,2} → length=3 → add [0,1,2]. Backtrack: remove 2, used={0,1}. No more cols → backtrack: remove 1, used={0}. Choose col=2: path=[0,2], used={0,2}. Recurse: choose col=1: path=[0,2,1] → add [0,2,1]. Continue similarly for starting col=1 and col=2.
Step 1: Start []. Step 2: Choose 0: path=[0], used={0}. Recurse: choose 1: path=[0,1] → add. Backtrack: choose 1 as start: path=[1], used={1}. Recurse: choose 0: path=[1,0] → add.

ladders.title: Building Block — Backtracking to Generate Sequences
ladders.desc: Use backtracking to generate all sequences of length N from a set of choices
ladders.explanation: Backtracking is a recursive technique to build sequences by making choices, exploring, and undoing choices. The intuition: We want to generate all possible arrangements (permutations) of N items. At each step, we choose an available item, add it to the current path, recurse to build the rest, then remove it to try other options. This ensures we explore all combinations without repetition. The algorithm: Base case: if path length equals N, record the path. Recursive case: iterate through all possible choices (0 to N-1), if choice not used, mark it used, add to path, recurse, then unmark and remove. Time complexity: O(N!) for permutations. Space: O(N) for recursion stack and storage. Connection to L2: This general backtracking is specialized to generate permutations for N-Queens. Common mistakes: Forgetting to backtrack (remove choices), not copying the path before storing, infinite recursion without base case. Real code pattern: def backtrack(path, used): if len(path)==N: results.append(path[:]); return; for i in range(N): if not used[i]: used[i]=True; path.append(i); backtrack(path, used); path.pop(); used[i]=False.

examples.trace: Step 1: path=[], used=[False,False]. Step 2: Choose i=0: used[0]=True, path=[0]. Recurse: len=1 < 2. Choose i=1: used[1]=True, path=[0,1]. Recurse: len=2 → add [0,1]. Backtrack: path pop to [0], used[1]=False. i loop ends, backtrack: path pop to [], used[0]=False. Step 3: Choose i=1: used[1]=True, path=[1]. Recurse: choose i=0: used[0]=True, path=[1,0] → add [1,0]. Backtrack similarly.

examples.output: 6 sequences (full trace similar to L2 example)
examples.trace: Similar to L2 example but focused on backtracking steps: Start [], choose 0 → [0], choose 1 → [0,1], choose 2 → [0,1,2] add, backtrack to [0,1], choose 2 already used, backtrack to [0], choose 2 → [0,2], choose 1 → [0,2,1] add, etc.

testCases.expected: [[0,0]] (if allowing repetition, but for permutations no)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if Two Points are on Same Diagonal
ladders.desc: Determine whether two queens (given row and column) threaten each other diagonally
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks if two points in a grid share a diagonal. The intuition: In a chessboard, points are on the same diagonal if the slope between them is ±1, meaning the absolute differences in rows and columns are equal. Mathematically, for points (r1,c1) and (r2,c2), they are on the same diagonal if |r1 - r2| = |c1 - c2|. This works because diagonals have constant row±column values. The algorithm is simple: compute row_diff = abs(r1 - r2), col_diff = abs(c1 - c2), return row_diff == col_diff. Time complexity: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in the diagonal conflict checker (L1) when comparing pairs of queens. Common mistakes: Forgetting absolute values, confusing rows and columns, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: def same_diagonal(r1,c1,r2,c2): return abs(r1 - r2) == abs(c1 - c2).

examples.trace: Compute row_diff = |0-1| = 1, col_diff = |0-1| = 1. Since 1 == 1, return True.
row_diff = |0-2| = 2, col_diff = |0-1| = 1. 2 != 1, return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Permutations
ladders.desc: Learn what a permutation is and how to count them
ladders.explanation: A permutation is an arrangement of items in a specific order. For N distinct items, there are N! (N factorial) permutations. Intuition: For the first position, you have N choices, for the second, N-1 choices, and so on, leading to N × (N-1) × ... × 1 = N!. This is foundational for the brute force N-Queens because we need to consider all ways to assign columns to rows. Example: For 3 items {A,B,C}, permutations are ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA. The algorithm to generate permutations involves selecting each item as the first, then recursively permuting the remaining. Time to list all: O(N!). Space: O(N) for storage. Connection to L4: Permutations provide the set of queen placements to check for diagonal conflicts. Common mistakes: Confusing permutations with combinations (where order doesn't matter), miscounting factorial. Real code pattern: Understand factorial growth: 1!=1, 2!=2, 3!=6, 4!=24, etc.

examples.trace: Step 1: Choose first item: 1 → permute remaining [2,3]: gives [2,3] and [3,2] → [1,2,3] and [1,3,2]. Step 2: Choose first item: 2 → permute [1,3]: [1,3] and [3,1] → [2,1,3] and [2,3,1]. Step 3: Choose first item: 3 → permute [1,2]: [1,2] and [2,1] → [3,1,2] and [3,2,1].
Step 1: Choose first: 0 → remaining [1] → [0,1]. Step 2: Choose first: 1 → remaining [0] → [1,0].

approaches.name: Bitmask Backtracking O(N!)
approaches.summary: Represent columns and diagonals as bitmasks for faster conflict checks using bitwise operations. Ideal for optimizing performance in languages that support efficient bit manipulation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — N-Queens with Bitmask Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of N-Queens using bitmask backtracking to find all valid board configurations.
ladders.explanation: The N-Queens problem requires placing N queens on an N×N chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. Using bitmask backtracking, we represent board constraints as bitmasks: columns, left diagonals (↖↘), and right diagonals (↗↙). Each bit position corresponds to a column or diagonal index. The intuition is that bitwise operations allow O(1) conflict checks instead of O(N) linear scans. We recursively place queens row by row, tracking occupied columns and diagonals via bitmasks. When placing a queen in a row, we iterate through available columns (bits set to 0 in the column mask), check diagonal conflicts using bitwise AND with diagonal masks, and if safe, set the corresponding bits and recurse. The base case is when all N queens are placed (row == N), at which point we record the board configuration. Time complexity is O(N!) due to the branching factor reducing by approximately 1 each row, though bitmask operations reduce constant factors. Space complexity is O(N) for recursion depth and storing solutions. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of checking and updating bitmasks for queen placement. Common mistakes include incorrect bit shifting for diagonals (e.g., forgetting to offset by row index) or mishandling bit indices (0-based vs 1-based). Real code pattern: Use integers for masks, with bitwise OR to set bits, AND to check conflicts, and left/right shifts for diagonal propagation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize masks: cols=0, ld=0, rd=0, row=0, board=["....","....","....","...."]. Step 2: Row 0: Available columns from bitmask ~(cols|ld|rd) & ((1<<4)-1) = 1111 (bits 0-3). Try col 0: Check conflicts: cols&(1<<0)=0, ld&(1<<0)=0, rd&(1<<0)=0 → safe. Update: cols=0001, ld=0010 (ld=(ld|1<<0)<<1), rd=0001 (rd=(rd|1<<0)>>1), board[0]="Q...". Recurse row=1. Step 3: Row 1: Available= ~(0001|0010|0001)&1111=1100 (cols 2,3). Try col 2: Check: cols&0100=0, ld&0100=0, rd&0100=0 → safe. Update: cols=0101, ld=1100 (ld=(0010|0100)<<1), rd=0010 (rd=(0001|0100)>>1), board[1]="..Q.". Recurse row=2. Step 4: Row 2: Available= ~(0101|1100|0010)&1111=0000 → backtrack. Step 5: Backtrack to row 1, try col 3: Check safe. Update: cols=1001, ld=1010 (ld=(0010|1000)<<1), rd=0101 (rd=(0001|1000)>>1), board[1]="...Q". Recurse row=2. Step 6: Row 2: Available= ~(1001|1010|0101)&1111=0100 (col 2). Check safe. Update: cols=1101, ld=0100 (ld=(1010|0100)<<1), rd=1010 (rd=(0101|0100)>>1), board[2]="..Q.". Recurse row=3. Step 7: Row 3: Available= ~(1101|0100|1010)&1111=0001 (col 0). Check safe. Update: cols=1101, ld=1000, rd=0101, board[3]="Q...". Recurse row=4: base case, record solution [".Q..","...Q","Q...","..Q."]. Step 8: Backtrack to explore other branches, find second solution similarly.

Step 1: Initialize masks: cols=0, ld=0, rd=0, row=0, board=["."]. Step 2: Row 0: Available columns= ~(0|0|0)&1=1 (col 0). Check safe. Update: cols=1, ld=0 (ld=(0|1)<<1=10 but masked to 0 for n=1), rd=0 (rd=(0|1)>>1=0), board[0]="Q". Step 3: Recurse row=1: base case, record solution [["Q"]].

testCases.expected: 40 solutions (list omitted for brevity, verified count)
92 solutions (list omitted for brevity, verified count)
352 solutions (list omitted for brevity, verified count)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bitmask Conflict Check and Update
ladders.desc: Function to check if a queen can be placed in a specific column using bitmasks and update masks after placement.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of bitmask backtracking, enabling efficient O(1) validation and state management. The intuition is to represent the board's occupied columns and diagonals as integer bitmasks, where each bit corresponds to a column or diagonal index. For an N×N board, we use N-bit masks (though integers typically have more bits). To check if placing a queen at (row, col) is safe: compute a bit position 'pos = 1 << col'. Then, check conflicts using bitwise AND: if (cols & pos) != 0, the column is occupied; if (ld & pos) != 0, the left diagonal (↖↘) is occupied; if (rd & pos) != 0, the right diagonal (↗↙) is occupied. If all are zero, the placement is safe. After placing, update masks: cols |= pos (set the column bit); ld |= pos, then ld <<= 1 (propagate left diagonal to next row); rd |= pos, then rd >>= 1 (propagate right diagonal to next row). Masking with ((1<<N)-1) ensures we only consider N bits. Time complexity is O(1) per check/update. Space is O(1) for masks. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called in each recursive step to validate and update state. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of bitmask representation. Common mistakes include not masking after shifts (causing bits to spill beyond N) or confusing diagonal directions. Real code pattern: Define a function safe_and_update(cols, ld, rd, col, n) that returns (new_cols, new_ld, new_rd) if safe, else None.

examples.input: n=4, row=0, col=1, cols=0, ld=0, rd=0
examples.trace: Step 1: pos = 1 << 1 = 2 (binary 0010). Step 2: Check conflicts: cols & 2 = 0, ld & 2 = 0, rd & 2 = 0 → safe. Step 3: Update: cols = 0 | 2 = 2 (0010). ld = (0 | 2) << 1 = 4 (0100). rd = (0 | 2) >> 1 = 1 (0001). Mask with (1<<4)-1=15 (1111): cols=2, ld=4, rd=1.

examples.input: n=4, row=1, col=2, cols=2, ld=4, rd=1
examples.trace: Step 1: pos = 1 << 2 = 4 (0100). Step 2: Check: cols & 4 = 2&4=0, ld & 4 = 4&4=4 → conflict! So placement is not safe. Output: None or indicate unsafe.

testCases.input: n=4, row=0, col=0, cols=0, ld=0, rd=0
n=4, row=0, col=3, cols=0, ld=0, rd=0
n=4, row=1, col=0, cols=1, ld=2, rd=0
n=4, row=1, col=2, cols=1, ld=2, rd=0
n=4, row=2, col=3, cols=5, ld=12, rd=1
n=5, row=0, col=2, cols=0, ld=0, rd=0
n=5, row=1, col=4, cols=4, ld=8, rd=2
n=5, row=2, col=1, cols=20, ld=2, rd=12
n=3, row=0, col=1, cols=0, ld=0, rd=0
n=3, row=1, col=0, cols=2, ld=4, rd=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Representing Board Constraints as Bitmasks
ladders.desc: Using integer bitmasks to encode occupied columns and diagonals for fast conflict detection.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of bitmask backtracking is to represent the chessboard's constraints—columns, left diagonals (↖↘), and right diagonals (↗↙)—as integer bitmasks. Each bit in these masks corresponds to an index: for columns, bit i represents column i; for diagonals, bit i represents diagonal i. The intuition is that bitwise operations (AND, OR, shifts) allow O(1) checks instead of O(N) linear scans over arrays. For an N×N board, we use N-bit masks, though integers provide more bits. Columns mask: a bit set to 1 indicates that column is occupied by a queen. Left diagonals mask: diagonals where row-col is constant; we map this to a bit index by adding an offset (e.g., row-col + N-1) to avoid negatives. Right diagonals mask: diagonals where row+col is constant. In practice, we avoid explicit mapping by propagating masks: when placing a queen at (row, col), we set the col bit in columns mask; for left diagonal, we set the col bit and shift left by 1 for the next row (since left diagonal moves down-left); for right diagonal, we set the col bit and shift right by 1 (since right diagonal moves down-right). This propagation automatically handles diagonal indices. Time and space are O(1) per mask operation. Connection to L1: This logic enables the conflict check subroutine. Connection to L3: It builds on bitwise operations. Common mistakes include incorrect shifting directions or not masking bits beyond N. Real code pattern: Maintain three integers cols, ld, rd; update as described.

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitwise Operations for Set Manipulation
ladders.desc: Using bitwise AND, OR, NOT, and shifts to manipulate sets of indices represented as bits.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using bitwise operations to handle sets of integers, where each bit in an integer represents an element's presence in the set. For an N-element set, we use an N-bit integer (though practical integers have more bits). Key operations: AND (&) checks for intersection (e.g., if a bit is set in both masks); OR (|) performs union (sets bits); NOT (~) complements bits; left shift (<<) and right shift (>>) move bits. In bitmask backtracking, we use these to manage columns and diagonals: e.g., available columns = ~(cols | ld | rd) & ((1<<N)-1) computes bits not set in any mask, masked to N bits. Setting a bit: mask |= (1 << i). Checking if bit i is set: (mask & (1 << i)) != 0. Shifting propagates diagonals. The intuition is that bitwise ops are extremely fast (O(1) hardware-level) compared to array operations. Time complexity is O(1) per operation; space is O(1) for the integer. Connection to L2: These operations implement the bitmask representation. Connection to L4: They rely on binary number concepts. Common mistakes include forgetting to mask after NOT (which yields negative numbers in two's complement) or using arithmetic shifts instead of logical shifts. Real code pattern: Define masks as integers; use bitwise ops with masking for safety.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute union: cols | ld | rd = 0010 | 0100 | 0001 = 0111 (binary). Step 2: Compute complement: ~0111 = ...11111000 (in two's complement, infinite 1s prefix). Step 3: Mask with (1<<4)-1 = 15 (1111): ...11111000 & 1111 = 1000. So available columns are bit 3 (col index 3).

examples.output: mask=10101, check bit 3=0, set bit 3 → mask=11101

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Number Manipulation
ladders.desc: Working with binary numbers: setting, clearing, and testing bits using powers of two.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves manipulating individual bits in binary numbers, which is essential for bitmask techniques. A binary number is a base-2 representation where each digit (bit) is 0 or 1, and positions represent powers of 2 (e.g., bit i corresponds to 2^i). To set bit i (make it 1): use number | (1 << i), where << is left shift (e.g., 1 << 3 = 8, binary 1000). To clear bit i (make it 0): use number & ~(1 << i), where ~ is bitwise NOT. To test if bit i is set: use (number & (1 << i)) != 0. To toggle bit i: use number ^ (1 << i). The intuition is that these operations allow efficient representation of sets or flags. Time and space are O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: This is the foundation for bitwise set manipulation. Connection to L5: It builds on binary number concepts. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in bit indices (0-based vs 1-based) or misunderstanding shift operations. Real code pattern: In Python, use integers and bitwise operators directly.

examples.trace: Step 1: 5 in binary is 101. Step 2: To set bit 1 (2^1=2), compute 1 << 1 = 2 (binary 010). Step 3: 5 | 2 = 101 | 010 = 111 (binary), which is 7 in decimal.

Step 1: 10 in binary is 1010. Step 2: Bit 2 corresponds to 2^2=4 (binary 0100). Step 3: 10 & 4 = 1010 & 0100 = 0000? Wait, 1010 & 0100 = 0000, so output is 0 (false). Correction: Bit positions: bit 0 is LSB. 1010: bits are 3:1, 2:0, 1:1, 0:0. Test bit 2: 1<<2=4 (0100), 1010 & 0100 = 0000 → false. So output should be 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bit Positions
ladders.desc: Understanding binary number system and how each bit represents a power of two.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: binary numbers are base-2 numerals using only digits 0 and 1. Each position in a binary number represents a power of 2, starting from 2^0 at the rightmost bit (least significant bit, LSB). For example, binary 1011 means 1*2^3 + 0*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 8+0+2+1 = 11 in decimal. Bit positions are typically indexed from 0 at the LSB. This foundation is crucial for bitmasking because we use bits to represent elements (e.g., columns on a chessboard), where a 1 indicates presence and 0 indicates absence. The intuition is that binary allows compact representation and fast operations. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. Connection to L4: This enables binary number manipulation. Common mistakes include confusing bit order (left vs right) or miscomputing decimal values. Real code pattern: In programming, integers are stored in binary; we can think in binary for bitwise ops.

examples.trace: Step 1: Write binary 110 with bit positions: bit 2:1, bit 1:1, bit 0:0. Step 2: Compute: 1*2^2 + 1*2^1 + 0*2^0 = 4 + 2 + 0 = 6.
Step 1: Convert 9 to binary: 9 = 8+1 = 2^3+2^0, so binary 1001. Step 2: Bit positions: bit 3:1, bit 2:0, bit 1:0, bit 0:1. Step 3: Bit 1 is 0.
